coredata.dc:title: Investigation of the influence of cell compression after winding on electrolyte wettability based on experiments and lattice Boltzmann simulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wanner, Johannes
Birke, Kai Peter
coredata.dc:description: The wetting of the active materials in a lithium-ion battery cell after electrolyte filling is a time-critical process in the manufacturing of lithium-ion batteries. The exact influencing factors on wetting are the subject of experimental investigations and numerical simulations. The dominant wetting at the material transitions, depending on the material’s compression caused by the cell winding, has not yet been examined. Therefore, this publication investigates the effects of material compression during the winding process on wettability through experiments and lattice Boltzmann simulations. The results show and quantify accelerated wetting caused in the material transitions compared to single electrode wetting, attributed to larger pores and higher permeability. Increasing compression in inner windings of cylindrical cells shows smaller pores sizes and reduced permeability, leading to a decelerated wetting behavior. Considering permeability and evaporation is essential for the mathematical replication of the wetting phenomena. With these findings, the total wetting time of a winding in a cylindrical 21700 cell can be estimated between 816 s to 1198 s.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Electrolyte wetting
Winding compression
Lattice Boltzmann method
Wetting time

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the pore size distribution
Pressure - saturation behavior of the geometry
Replication of the experimental wetting process
Horizontal wetting of the materials and complete cell wetting

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Federal Ministry of Education and Research, Germany, for funding this work as part of the accompanying research project “VisEl—Visualisierung der Elektrolytbewegungen und Elektrodenausdehnung einer Lithium-Ionen-Zelle”, funding code 03XP0529D.

This research was funded by the Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) , funding code 03XP0529D . The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Federal Ministry of Education and Research, Germany , for funding this work as part of the accompanying research project “VisEl—Visualisierung der Elektrolytbewegungen und Elektrodenausdehnung einer Lithium-Ionen-Zelle”.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microscopy image of the used graphite anode (a) and separator (b).

The experimental setup consists of a electrode and separator (1) placed on each other and compressed together via two glass plates (2). The compression is set with four screws (3) and four metal plates (4) using a torque wrench. The separator can be seen in the foreground of the cell stack and the anode in the background. Wetting is triggered by an electrolyte application (5) on the glass plate (6) below and recorded by an industrial camera (7).
Resulting pore size distribution based on the microscopy image.

Winding stress as a function of the 24 windings of a 21700 cylindrical cell and the resulting reduction in thickness of the separator. Winding #1 corresponds to the inner, and winding #24 to the outer winding.

Wetting state of winding #20 at t = 186 s. The white area corresponds to the non-wetted materials, and the dark gray area to the completely wetted material. The light gray area corresponds to a partial wetting of the anode. The image evaluation is done with a small section in the middle of the picture. The dashed lines are the wetting height markers from the image recognition. The resolution of the images is
49
μ m
.

The wetting height of the anode and the stacked materials according to winding #20, #9, and #5 is plotted versus time. The resolution of 49 μ m enables an exact determination of the wetting level. Sources of error arise due to the slightly fluctuating brightness values of the pixels, which are compensated by a running average. Due to the compression of the glass plates at the edge of the structure, the contact pressure is not distributed exactly evenly. This leads to lower compression, particularly with winding #5 from t = 300 s, and therefore to accelerated wetting.
Resulting pore size distribution based on the simulation of winding #24.

Results of the simulation of the electrolyte saturation over the capillary pressure for different windings. Saturation is measured absolutely for each voxel of the geometry. The mean value of the gas and electrolyte density of y = 2 is used to calculate the capillary pressure. The heterogeneous geometric shape of the electrode results in a deviation of 2.6% from the maximum possible capillary pressure and 17 % from the minimum capillary pressure. A local breakdown of the capillary pressure was carried out in previous work [14].

Comparison of the experimental and replicated wetting of anode, winding #20, #9, and #5 with the Eqs. (6) and (5) with different input parameters. Sources of error arise due to the slightly fluctuating brightness values of the pixels, which are compensated by a running average.
Illustration of a wetting process at the material transition and subsequent horizontal wetting between the electrode and separator.
Properties and measurement results of the used anode and separator.
The wetting rate and effective pore radii as calculated from the experiments with Eq. (6) .
Simulation results of different winding’s pore size distribution, permeability and porosity.
Overview of the horizontal wetting parameters of the individual materials. The parameters to be determined are listed in bold.
Overview of the physical parameterization of the electrolyte (E) and gas (G).
Conversion factors between SI units and LBM units (lu = length unit, ts = time step, mu = mass unit).

head.ce:title: Investigation of the influence of cell compression after winding on electrolyte wettability based on experiments and lattice Boltzmann simulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fraunhofer Institute for Manufacturing Engineering and Automation IPA, Nobelstr. 12, Stuttgart, 70569, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fraunhofer Institute for Manufacturing Engineering and Automation IPA

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Fraunhofer Institute for Manufacturing Engineering and Automation IPA, addressline=Nobelstr. 12, city=Stuttgart, postcode=70569, country=Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Photovoltaics, Chair of Electrical Energy Storage Systems, University of Stuttgart, Pfaffenwaldring 47, Stuttgart, 70569, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Photovoltaics, Chair of Electrical Energy Storage Systems, University of Stuttgart

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Institute for Photovoltaics, Chair of Electrical Energy Storage Systems, University of Stuttgart, addressline=Pfaffenwaldring 47, city=Stuttgart, postcode=70569, country=Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The wetting of the active materials in a lithium-ion battery cell after electrolyte filling is a time-critical process in the manufacturing of lithium-ion batteries. The exact influencing factors on wetting are the subject of experimental investigations and numerical simulations. The dominant wetting at the material transitions, depending on the material’s compression caused by the cell winding, has not yet been examined. Therefore, this publication investigates the effects of material compression during the winding process on wettability through experiments and lattice Boltzmann simulations. The results show and quantify accelerated wetting caused in the material transitions compared to single electrode wetting, attributed to larger pores and higher permeability. Increasing compression in inner windings of cylindrical cells shows smaller pores sizes and reduced permeability, leading to a decelerated wetting behavior. Considering permeability and evaporation is essential for the mathematical replication of the wetting phenomena. With these findings, the total wetting time of a winding in a cylindrical 21700 cell can be estimated between 816 s to 1198 s.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The lithium-ion battery is used in different applications and is mass-produced in various formats around the globe. The main goals to reduce costs and environmental impact can be achieved by minimizing process times and improving product quality. This links different cell formats and applications [1,2]. Through digitalization, mass-produced battery cells can be individually manufactured to achieve these goals [3,4]. The process times are standardized and selected empirically, especially after cell assembly in cell finishing [5,6]. Due to the closed metallic housing of the battery cell, the process phenomena are complex to access, but can be estimated by a digital twin considering the physical phenomena of the specific processes. The wetting step after electrolyte filling is widely discussed in the literature [7]. Here, capillary forces wet the dry porous battery materials with the liquid electrolyte. The phenomena are sufficiently recognized and can be represented mathematically, but parameterization is complicated [8,9]. Thus, the total wetting time is difficult to estimate, and experimental analysis is time-consuming and cannot be used inline [7]. Nevertheless, it is possible to examine the entire cell or individual materials and their combinations in the laboratory using electrical or optical methods [7]. These methods are used to parameterize mathematical models to estimate wetting, taking the process framework conditions into account [5]. Numerical fluid dynamics simulations, particularly the lattice Boltzmann method (LBM), provide support to simulate capillary wetting in small dimensions [10–12].

The different experimental methods and LBM simulations show heterogeneous results of wetting speed. In particular, investigating individual battery materials and entire cells varies greatly. For the mathematical replication of wetting, the influences of the process conditions are known; the wetting properties of the porous battery materials are often unknown. Studies of individual materials and material combinations show different wetting properties because the surface properties of individual materials play a significant role in the combination of materials in a cell stack [13,14]. Using the optical method of the capillary rise test, Kaden et al. show an acceleration of wetting by laminating the electrode surface and an influence of the electrode-separator combination on wetting [15]. Beyer et al. used gravimetric and optical methods to investigate the wetting of individual materials and complete stacks. They underline the importance of considering the morphology and surface parameters of the materials to understand the wetting of material interfaces [16]. Furthermore, the material’s main pore radius is insufficient to mathematically simulate the wetting of electrodes with different porosity as shown by Sheng et al. [17]. Schilling et al. used a pressing device to accelerate electrolyte wetting by compressing a pouch cell [18]. Davoodabadi et al. investigated the wetting of differently calendered electrodes. The wetting speed decelerates with increasing calendering [19]. Generally, in addition to the pore radius, other coefficients like permeability or tortuosity are introduced and determined by experiments to replace the wetting of the electrode or separator [20,21]. Günter et al. present a mathematical model to describe wetting at the cell level. The material properties are determined across materials based on optical investigations using neutron radiography of wound prismatic cells [8,22]. Hagemeister et al. reproduces this optical wetting process numerically. Simulating the process framework conditions concerning the influencing factors of the electrodes and separator geometry can verify the optical investigations at the cell level [23]. Chen et al. investigated the wetting process of a wound prismatic cell with X-ray and measured a reduced wetting speed of the highly compressed inner windings. Here, the total cell was compressed with 0.1 and 0.2 MPa. This leads to different wetting properties of the separator, which a numerical simulation can confirm. The exact influencing factors of the porous media in the cell are not described [24].

Lee and Jeon investigated the effect of compressing an electrode by calendering with an LBM simulation. A more compressed electrode leads to decelerated wetting [25]. Shodiev et al. investigated the effects of calendering and the resulting wetting of a stack of anode, separator, and cathode by LBM. Here, the compression within the electrode due to calendering is considered, but not the compression between the materials. As a result, Shodiev et al. quantify the pore size distribution, porosity, and tortuosity of the stochastically generated geometries [12,26]. Shin et al. investigated the anode-separator transitions by simulation. The separator geometry is created here based on focused ion beam scanning electron microscope images. No compression of the materials is investigated either, but the small pores of the separator result in large gas entrapments in the geometry [27]. Lautenschlaeger et al. quantify the permeability, porosity, pore size, and gas entrapments for different LBM simulation runs to evaluate the wetting [11].

The literature shows that knowledge of the material properties of porosity, pore radius and permeability is necessary for the analytical simulation of experimental wetting. All of the literature cited can only quantify some of the possible influencing factors in the experiments [28]. This prevents transferability to other material systems [29]. The simulation of the wetting phenomena enables the determination of material properties. The simulations in the literature without experimental validation cannot make any statements about the wetting duration of a cell due to the small observation scale. In addition, an investigation of individual materials and the combination of different materials shows different wetting properties that arise due to the material transitions. These material transitions depend on the cell design and production process. These effects must, therefore, be taken into account. The literature cited shows a gap in considering the material transitions specifically from the point of view of the compression of the materials. In particular, [24] has found a clear correlation between the wetting and the compression of a coil in a prismatic cell. Since the cell-level results are greatly simplified, this publication deals with investigating the influence of cell compression of cylindrical cells after winding on the wetting of the individual materials. This is done using the capillary rise method to allow optical quantification of the wetting phenomena. Furthermore, the exact influencing factors of the pore sizes, permeability, and gas entrapments will be investigated using a lattice Boltzmann simulation.

Material-independent, the Lucas–Washburn equation is suitable to describe the capillary rise in porous media [30]. The wetting is mainly triggered by the capillary pressure
P
C
. With porous media, such as battery materials, the loss of viscosity due to Darcy’s law (
∇
P
V
) must also be taken into account. The hydrostatic pressure (
P
H
) and ambient pressure (
p
0
) are relevant for the considered time span of the observation the capillary effect [31].
(1)
d h
d t
=
∑ P
8

r

2
)
, with: ∑ P =
P
C
−
P
H
− ∇

V

p
0
,
where the height of liquid penetration represents h , and
μ
L
denotes the liquid’s dynamic viscosity. The specific porous media with variable pore radii can be described by an effective pore radius distribution
r
eff
. The capillary pressure
P
C
is defined as
(2)
P
C
=
2 γ cos
( θ )

eff
.
The surface tension between the liquid and the gas γ , and the contact angle between the liquid and solid surface θ cause the capillary wetting. The hydrostatic pressure of the liquid is defined as
(3)
P
h,L

ρ

,
where ρ describes the density of the respective fluid, g the gravity, and ψ the alignment angle of the porous media in relation to a horizontal axis. The viscous pressure loss
∇
P
V,L
of the liquid is corresponds to
(4)

μ

k
,
where k describes to the permeability [31]. These pressure-influencing factors can be further extended if capillary acceleration, in particular, is considered for small periods of time. In this work, the atmospheric pressure and the permeability of the air in the pores is neglected, and the formula defined to:
(5)
d h
d t
=
r
eff
2
k
E

E
μ
E

μ
E

eff
2
)
⋅
[
2 γ cos
( θ )
r
eff
−
ρ
E
⋅ g ⋅ h ⋅ sin
( ψ )
]
.
In the literature, the Washburn equation is usually greatly simplified, without the Darcy law for porous media and only expressed with a wetting factor K in mm ⋅ s
− 0 . 5
, which is defined as follows:
(6)
h = K ⋅
t
, K =
γ
2 η
⋅ cos
( θ )
⋅
r
eff
.
The following experiments are evaluated using both Eq. (5) and Eq. (6) to compare the different Equations.

A graphite anode and a Celgard 2500 separator are used in the experiments. The materials are examined using the Keyence VK-9710 laser scanning microscope (Keyence, Osaka, Japan). The images are shown in Fig. 1. These materials are characterized by surface roughness, porosity, average particle, and pore sizes.

The wetting material used is dimethyl carbonate (DMC) from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), a main component of a lithium-ion electrolyte. The material properties of DMC, including surface tension (
γ = 32 . 01 ⋅ 1
0
− 3
N/m), dynamic viscosity (
μ = 1 . 464 ⋅ 1
0
− 3
Pa ⋅
s) and density (
ρ = 1070
kg/
m
3
), are based on the manufacturer’s specifications and literature values [32–35]. An optical contact angle measurement and contour analysis system OCA 40 from DataPhysics Instruments GmbH (Filderstadt, Germany) determines the contact angle between the DMC and the anode or separator. The porosity and pore radius of the separator are taken from [13]. The results are shown in Table 1.

The separator and the electrode are placed on each other and compressed together using two glass plates. The compression of the cell stack is set with four screws and two metal plates at the edge of the electrode/separator stack using a torque wrench. The width of the materials is always 47 mm. For initial wetting, the stack protrudes by 2 mm at the bottom of the glass plates. Therefore, the glass plates do not touch the DMC, and there is no unwanted capillary effect between the glass plates. The construction is held vertically by a laboratory holder, 1 mm above another glass plate, and 1 ml of DMC is dosed onto the glass plate placed below the electrode stack for each experiment. In addition to the cell stack, only the anode is wetted in the same procedure. However, the glass plates are omitted here so as not to influence the wetting. The setup is illuminated with LEDs to record the wetting. A specific industrial camera, DFK 33GX183 from The Imaging Source, LLC (Charlotte, NC, USA), records the wetting process with a sample rate of two frames per second and a resolution of 20 megapixels. The setup is shown in Fig. 2. The experiments are conducted at a temperature of 20
° C
. Data evaluation is processed with Matlab R2023a to analyze sharpness, contrast, and gray values and quantify the wetting level at the stack.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the pore size distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The pore sizes can be determined with the Porespy library using the laser scanning microscopy image of the anode in Fig. 1a [36]. For this purpose, the image is converted into a binary image, and the largest possible local spheres are calculated into the geometry for each voxel. The pore size distribution is subsequently determined. This is shown in Fig. 3 below:

The pores do not correspond to a normal distribution. The median of the pore size distribution is
0 . 957
μ m
. 20.1% of the pores are in the range between
0 . 129
μ m
and
0 . 3
μ m
. The remaining pores are relatively evenly distributed between
0 . 8
μ m
and
4 . 94
μ m
. This method only quantifies the pores on the surface of the electrode and not the pores in the electrode between the graphite grains.

A winding radial stress (
σ
R
w
) model based on [37] is used to determine the compression of the battery materials during the winding process:
(7)
σ
R
w
=
∫
R
0
R
max
−
T
P
(
R
w
)
R
max
2
R
max
2
+
R
0
2
( 1 −
R
0
2
R
w
2
)
d
R
w
, with:
T
P
(
R
w
)
=
F
(
R
w
)
d ⋅ h
,
where
R
0
is the radius of the winding mandrel,
T
P
( R )
the winding tension,
R
max
the final radius of the jelly roll. Here, a winding mandrel with
R
0
= 2
mm
and an outer radius of
R = 10
mm
is assumed. The thickness of the total winding is assumed to be
d = 325
μ m
, and the height of the jelly roll is
h = 65
mm
. This results in a jelly roll with 24 windings. The tensile force during winding is initially set at
F
(
R
w
= 1 )
= 10
N and decreases linearly by 0.36 N in each winding [38]. The resulting radial winding stress of each winding is shown in Fig. 4. Taking into account the bulk modulus of graphite (34 GPa [39]) and the separator (82 MPa [40]), the compression in the materials can be calculated; here, simplified only the change in thickness. As the graphite’s compression modulus is significantly higher than the separator’s, it is assumed that only the separator is compressed. This thickness reduction of the separator due to the compression caused by winding is also shown in Fig. 4.

The radial winding pressure is set planar to the glass surface with a defined torque, taking into account the screw geometry. In the following, the winding tension is set as it acts on the winding #5, #9, and #20.

Fig. 5 shows an example of a wetting process with winding #20 at t = 186 s. The non-wetted areas are white, the partially wetted areas are light gray, and the fully wetted areas are dark gray. This difference in the gray areas can be observed in all experiments of an anode separator stack. After a short interval, the light gray area changes to dark gray. This interval can be explained by an initial wetting of the anode with subsequent wetting of the separator. The interval between partial and total wetting increases with increasing wetting height. This effect can also be observed to a lesser degree in Kaden et al. [15] and is further discussed in Section 3.7. A small section of 30 pixels in the middle of the stack over the entire height is used for image evaluation.

In Fig. 6, the wetting heights are plotted as a function of time. The wetting progress of the anode is slower compared to the compressed cell stacks. Additionally, a saturation of the anode wetting can be observed from
t > 300
s. The materials in a compressed state are always wet to the maximum height of the materials. The compressed materials exhibit a similar course; in particular, winding numbers #9 and #5 are close to each other. The wetting of the anode, shown here with a dashed line, occurs faster than the total material wetting, shown with a solid line in all cell stacks. This interval increases with the winding tension. A relatively linear increase can be observed for windings #9 and #20. For winding #5, an acceleration of the wetting can be observed from t = 300 s. This effect is caused by less intense compression in this area of the glass plate and is, therefore, not considered for further evaluation.

The wetting rate K is determined according previous findings and Kaden et al. [10,14,15]. In all experiments, the wetting shows a linear progression for the square root of time between t = 30 to 80 s. During this period, the wetting rate K is determined with a high accuracy of rmse
> 0 . 971
. Eq. (6) can be used to determine the effective pore radius of each experiment. The results are shown in Table 2.

The anode’s wetting rate and the calculated effective pore radii are smaller than the wetting rate and pore radii of the compressed cell stacks. Furthermore, the cell stack’s wetting rate and calculated pore radii decrease with increasing compression. The calculated values of the anode do not correspond to the pore size distribution on the surface of the electrode in Fig. 3. However, the pore size distribution represents the calculated pore radii of the windings #20, #9, and #5.

The procedure and setup are adopted and adapted from previous work [10,14]. The LBM simulation is carried out in the open-source environment Palabos [41]. The parameterization is listed in Tables A.5 and A.6. The simulation results are evaluated in ParaView 5.11 and Matlab R2023a. The geometries are examined for their effective pore radius, permeability, and pressure-wetting behavior. The voxel size of a lattice unit corresponds to
45 . 9
nm
.

The 3D geometry of the electrode is extracted based on the microscopy image, utilizing the height information encoded in the color of the images. For this purpose, a 2D Gaussian filter is applied to the image, and the hue value of the HSV colors is correlated with the height of the respective pixel. The total height of the geometry corresponds to twice the mean surface roughness
S
A
. The resolution is
45 . 9
nm
. This results in a total dimension of the electrode geometry of (
28 . 4
×
19 . 4
×
6 . 06
μ
m
3
). The bottom layer is permeable to gas. The resulting geometry is shown in Figs. A.11 and A.12. The 3D geometry of the separator is created with the gray value levels of the microscopy image. Since the grey value of a pixel corresponds to the thickness of the separator fiber, the value of the HSV colors is used to create a
183 . 6
nm
thick fiber. Since this only corresponds to the half-cylinder of fiber, it is mirrored on the center axis. The resulting fiber thickness corresponds to
367 . 2
nm
thick and is rotated and stacked mirrored on each other. During stacking, an overlap with a logical AND of the facing fibers is considered. As a result, an average fiber of the geometry corresponds to the measured fiber diameter. The final geometry size corresponds to (
28 . 4
×
19 . 4
×
1 . 83
μ
m
3
) with a porosity of 53 %. The geometries of the separator and the electrode are added in the z-direction. Since the separator is pliable, a simple addition of the geometries would not correspond to reality. Therefore, the transition is realized as a logical AND of the upper layers of both geometries. This results in variable contact surfaces of the geometries depending on the contact pressure. For the thickness changes of the separator described in Fig. 4, an overlap of the separator with the anode between
0 . 035
μ m
for winding #24 and
1 . 36
μ m
for winding #1 is set. The resulting pore size, permeability, and pressure-saturation-wetting are evaluated.
The distance between the separator and the anode for winding #24 is evaluated using Matlab and depicted in the following pore size distribution shown in Fig. 7.

It can be observed that the pore sizes are normally distributed, with
μ = 2 . 38
μ m
and
σ = 1 . 43
μ m
. Pores below
0 . 19
μ m
are clustered due to the resolution of the geometry. A peak at
5 . 21
μ m
is also recognizable, corresponding approximately to
μ + 2 σ
of the normal distribution. When compared to the measured pore size distribution from the microscopy image in Fig. 3, the pore size distribution of the geometry is of the same size order. However, the mean pore size is more than twice as high (
μ = 2 . 38
μ m
compared to
μ = 0 . 958
μ m
in Fig. 3). A peak with larger pore sizes is evident in both cases (
5 . 21
μ m
compared to
4 . 94
μ m
). These values are, therefore, of particular interest in the following. In general, except for the deviation for small radii below
0 . 3
μ m
, the geometry corresponds to a realistic simulation of the surface properties of the electrode.

The relative permeability k describes the ability of a fluid to pass through a porous media. This is incorporated in the analytical Darcy Eq. (4) of viscous pressure loss for the liquid or gas. To determine the relative permeability of a porous media, the lattice Boltzmann setup is filled with a fluid, and an external force density of
f
x
= 5
⋅ 1
0
− 4
luts
− 2
is initiated along the x-direction. Using the average flow velocity through the pores in x-direction and the kinetic viscosity of the fluid, the relative permeability can be calculated as [11,41]:
(8)
k
G,E
=
μ
G,E
⋅
u
x
¯
f
x
.
According to Fries et al. the porosity of the porous media can be calculated based on the pore size and permeability:
(9)
ϕ =
8
k
E
r
eff
2
.
Based on the specified porosity of 25% and the median of the pore size distribution of the microscopic image of
r
eff
= 0 . 957
μ m
, a permeability of
k
E
= 2 . 86 ⋅ 1
0
− 14
m
2
for the anode can be calculated. For the separator, the permeability is determined as
k
E
= 5 . 2 ⋅ 1
0
− 15
m
2
, based on
r
eff
= 275
nm
and a porosity of 55%. The resulting geometries of the individual windings are evaluated in the following Table 3 considering pore size, permeability, and porosity. Winding #1 corresponds to the inner winding, winding #24 to the outer winding. The simulation reveals a decrease in pore sizes with increasing compression in inner windings. Moreover, fewer permeabilities can be simulated, leading to lower porosities due to Eq. (9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pressure - saturation behavior of the geometry
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the wetting behavior of different windings’ material transitions, a pressure-saturation simulation is conducted in the y-direction. For this purpose, pressure boundary conditions of the two fluids
ρ
E
and
ρ
dis
G
, are specified for the geometry. A reservoir with a membrane which is permeable to the electrolyte at the inlet and permeable to the gas at the outlet, is added in the y-direction. At the beginning, the geometry is defined with gas
ρ
G
and electrolyte density
ρ
dis
E
. The contact angles between the fluids and the geometry are implemented using a bounce-back function for the respective geometry with different adhesion parameters,
G
ads,wetting
for the electrolyte, and
G
ads,non-wetting
for the gas. The wetting process occurs through the pressure difference of the fluid phases, the interaction parameter
G
inter
, and the time step
Δ t
[11,42]:
(10)
P =
1
3
[ ρ +
ρ
dis
]
+
1
3
[
G
inter
⋅ ρ ⋅
ρ
dis
⋅ Δ
t
2
]
,
(11)
Δ P =
P
inlet
−
P
outlet
.
This pressure difference corresponds to the capillary pressure and is stimulated via an external force at layer y = 1 in the y-direction. This force is evaluated and increased every 500 time steps. A lattice cell is considered wetted when the gas density falls below
ρ
G
< 1
mu/lu
3
. The corresponding saturation degree is calculated from the ratio of the sum of wetted lattice cells to the total lattice cells that are filled with gas at the beginning.

In Fig. 8 the wetting process as a function of the capillary pressure acting on windings #20, #9, and #5 is plotted. Wetting is initiated almost equally at 0.61 MPa in all three cases. At 0.63 MPa, an increase in wetting can be seen at the beginning in all three cases. Subsequently, a linear wetting progression can be observed. Winding #20 rises flatter than the almost congruent windings #9 and #5. At 0.9 MPa, no further wetting can be observed in windings #9 and #5, indicating a final gas entrapment of 14.5%. The wetting of winding #20 ends at a capillary pressure of 0.94 MPa, resulting in gas entrapment of 7.4%. Since only the pore radius and permeability of the geometries, are variable in this simulation, Eq. (2) confirms that the geometry with winding #20 has a higher pore radius.

Sauter et al. indicate that gas entrapment in porous media occurs particularly when pores of different sizes are located parallel to each other in the geometry. In this case, the fluid rises faster in the smaller pore and encloses the air in the parallel larger pore. However, the essential geometric factor of permeability is not considered here [32]. Lautenschläger disagrees with this thesis and emphasizes that the position of the particles and pores is relevant. Smaller pores have smaller volumes, resulting in smaller gas entrapments in the geometry. Gas entrapments are mainly located in tiny pores and corners limited by active material and occur more frequently at low permeability [11]. The simulations conducted by Wanner and Birke confirm the assumptions made by Lautenschlaeger et al. [14]. Shodiev also supports this thesis and underscores the close relationship between the wettability of electrodes and their porosity, the pore size distribution, tortuosity factor, and the organization of the pore network. The impact of calendering on the gas entrapments is also considered significant. In this case, electrode compression reduces the pore size, increases the number of pores enclosed by active material, and slows down wettability [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Replication of the experimental wetting process
ce:section.ce:para:
The median of the pore distribution from the laser scanning microscope image and the calculated permeability are employed to replicate the wetting progress of the anode. The pore size distribution, specifically
r
eff
= μ
and
μ + 2 σ
along the permeability from the LBM simulation of the respective winding, serve as input parameters for Eq. (5). Subsequently, the resulting wetting is compared with the experiments for each winding in Fig. 9. Additionally, the replication using Eq. (6) is illustrated, and the calculated wetting rates are presented in Table 2 for comparison:

The wetting of the anode cannot be accurately represented solely with the wetting rate K. Eq. (6), which is based on pore radius and permeability, represents wetting up to 40 s. Subsequently, the experimental wetting increases slowly and then remains constant. Meanwhile, the calculated wetting continues to increase. In general, evaporation is inevitable in experiments involving volatile fluids in the atmosphere. In such cases, evaporation can be recognized by a rapid increase in wetting and followed by a subsequent asymptotic progression. Fries introduces an additional wetting height-dependent term into Eq. (5) [43]:
(12)
P
evap
=
m
̇
e
2
2
( w + t )
w ⋅ t
μ
E
ρ
E
k
E
h
2
,
where
m
̇
e
describes the normalized evaporation rate, w the width, and t the thickness of the porous media immersed in the liquid. An evaporation rate of
2 . 69 ⋅ 1
0
− 4
kg/m
2
s can be determined for this measurement’s geometric and fluid conditions and is also shown in the figure (see Fig. 9).

The wetting progress of winding #20 is well replicated by all Equations up to a t = 50 s. Up to 100 s, a good replication using Eq. (5) can also be observed. Thereafter, the experimental wetting progresses faster than the calculated wetting. The wetting replication using the wetting rate K in Eq. (6) corresponds approximately to the wetting in Eq. (5) with
R
eff
= μ + 2 σ
, although the pore radius is smaller by a factor of 5. The wetting process in winding #9 can be well replicated by Eq. (5). Together with the dashed line, this curve with larger pore radii frames the experimental wetting process. The wetting according to Eq. (6) corresponds to the experimental curve at the beginning of wetting up to 100 s, which can be explained by determining the wetting rate in this time period (Section 3.4). According to Eq. (5), the large pore radii can reproduce winding #5 excellently (dashed line). Eqs. (5) and (6) are congruent and deviate from the experiments. The effective pore radii differ significantly in these two equations, again, by a factor of approximately 5.

The wetting curves with the wetting rate K of Eq. (6) differ from the experiments above
t > 100
s. Furthermore, the calculated pore radii do not correspond to the simulations. The calculated pore radii in the windings are plausible, as verified in Fig. 3, but are at the lower boundary. With Eq. (5) and significantly larger pore radii, the permeability enables a more precise replication of the wetting progress of the cell stack. Therefore, large pores occur more in the material combination of the anode and separator. This is underlined by considering the pore radii of
r
eff
= μ
and
r
eff
= μ + 2 σ
, which together frame the experimental wetting process in windings #9 and #5. Furthermore, considering the material’s permeability is necessary for a precise replication of the wetting progress. For the consideration of individual materials in the atmosphere, Equation
? ? ?
must also be extended to include evaporation to achieve a good replication of wetting. No evaporation is observed for the experiments in the compressed state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Horizontal wetting of the materials and complete cell wetting
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the visual measurements of gradual wetting in Fig. 6, the horizontal wetting (y-axis) is of importance. Eq. (5) with an angle
ψ = 0
is also evaluated with the material parameters. In order to eliminate the dynamic effects at the beginning of wetting, the wetting height after
160
s from the experiments is used as the starting condition. The effective pore radius of the compressed separator is determined based on the experimental findings. For this purpose, the calculated variation in thickness due to the pressing and the visually recorded delayed wetting of the entire cell stack are used for parameterization.

Additionally, the same method is used to calculate the time required to wet a material horizontally without compression. The maximum height of a jelly roll (65 mm) is used as the starting height for wetting. The manufacturer’s values for the pore radius of the separator and the calculated permeability and pore radius of the anode are used for this purpose. The exact parameters are listed in Table 4:

The wetting of the separator during compression takes place between 7.5 and 28 s, according to the experiments. With the permeability from the simulation, small pore radii between 63 and 9.25 nm can be calculated. These calculated effective pore radii of the separator are much smaller than the specified pore radii of the raw material. Compression has a strong influence on the resulting pore radii. The horizontal wetting time between 6.5 and 8.2 s for the separator and anode corresponds to winding #9 and #20.

The wetting time of winding #5 up to a height of 65 mm with Eq. (5) and
r
eff
= μ
and
r
eff
= μ + 2 σ
amounts to 808 s to 1190 s. For winding #20, it is 613 s to 755 s. Combined with the anode wetting at a height of 65 mm, a total wetting time of 816.2 s to 1198.2 s is calculated for a cylindrical 21700 cell. However, the influence of the cathode is not taken into account here. For this purpose, the presented procedure can be repeated and compared with the anode wetting. If the winding tension is reduced to
F
(
R
w
= 1 )
= 5 Nm during the winding process, the thickness reduction of the separator is reduced to 2.7% in the inner winding, corresponding to winding #16 in the investigated case. This leads to a wetting time between 731 s and 1051 s. If only the individual material, in this case, the anode, is considered to calculate the wetting time of a jelly roll, Eq. (5) without evaporation results in a duration of 3810 s, and Eq. (6) results in a duration of 9788 s up to a height of 65 mm. The wetting times vary greatly and are significantly higher than the calculated wetting time, considering the material transitions.

A comparison with the literature shows comparable results of this study: Günther et al. determined a pore radius of 5 μ m for the wetting of an entire prismatic cell, corresponding to the dominating calculated
μ + 2 σ
pore radius of this study [8]. Kaden et al. verified the order of magnitude of the wetting rates K for individual materials neglecting evaporation [15]. Beyer et al. supported the findings that combinations of electrode/separator material exhibit faster wetting than individual components [16]. However, Davoodabadi et al. explored various calendering pressures and established the precise relationship between pressure and wetting speed [19]. They emphasized the importance of considering not only the pore radius but also permeability as an influencing parameter of wetting [20,21]. Furthermore, the free surface energy and surface area fraction influence the wetting properties of the electrode and separator [44]. This can be taken into account in further work.

In summary, the wetting process of a compressed material stack can be described in Fig. 10, dominated by wetting at the material transition. This results from the larger pore sizes and greater permeability at the material transitions. Horizontal wetting can be observed and quantified depending on the wetting height at the transition. The wetting speed, resulting pore radii, and the permeability of the material transition and the separator strongly depend on the external pressure applied to the materials by compression in the jelly roll or the pouch cell.

In accordance with the design of a 21700 jelly roll, the wetting of the compressed electrode and separator is investigated experimentally and by LBM simulation. It was shown experimentally that wetting in the compressed cell stack results in greater permeability due to the larger pores on the surface of the electrode and faster wetting compared to single electrode wetting. As the compression of the materials increases, wetting decelerates due to the reduction in the pore sizes at the transition of the materials. These effects are determined by quantifying the pore sizes at the electrode surface and underpinned by an electrode/separator model taking compression into account. In addition, the influence of the permeability of this material transition can be demonstrated, providing a more precise mathematical description of wetting. The wetting rate is found to be unsuitable for the mathematical description of wetting for larger time points
t > 100
s. For individual materials in the atmosphere, the evaporation rate emerges a key parameter for describing wetting. Utilizing these key findings along with the fluid properties, it becomes feasible to mathematically describe the experimental wetting process. Horizontal wetting of the materials can also be quantified. Particularly, the experiments reveal that the horizontal wetting of the separator slows down as the compression of the materials increases. This method estimates the total wetting time of the cylindrical jelly roll between 816 s and 1198 s. In the future, this information can be employed as a digital twin of the wetting process to determine the individual wetting time of the electrodes used in the cell.

The authors would like to express their gratitude to the Federal Ministry of Education and Research, Germany , for funding this work as part of the accompanying research project “VisEl—Visualisierung der Elektrolytbewegungen und Elektrodenausdehnung einer Lithium-Ionen-Zelle”, funding code 03XP0529D.

Johannes Wanner: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kai Peter Birke: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to Fabian Trost for setting up the first experimental setup and conducting basic measurements. They also extend their thanks to Moira Peter for her valuable input and support in setting up the lattice Boltzmann simulation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para:
In the LBM simulation the multicomponent Shan–Chen method is used. The adhesion parameter
G
ads
E,G
is calculated according to [45]. The parameterization represents dimethyl carbonate (DMC) as the electrolyte and air as gas. In this work the Reynolds number of
R e
=
1
⋅
1
0
− 3
can be determined in the experiments and simulations. The capillary number corresponds to
C a
=
1 . 2
⋅
1
0
− 6
. The conversion factors between lattice and SI units are provided in the table below:

803525 Sigma-aldrich dimethylcarbonat: Dimethyl carbonate for synthesis. CAS 616-38-6, chemical formula (CH
3
O)
2
